Endocrine Pancreas & Diabetes Pharmacology

Dr. Ochs OMK Block 5 · Week 40 Pharmacology 20 Learning Objectives
Theme
Text 100%
LO 1 Identify the cell types within the pancreatic islets of Langerhans and name the major hormone secreted by each cell type

🔬 Pancreatic Islet Cell Types

The Islets of Langerhans are clusters of endocrine cells scattered throughout the pancreas (concentrated in the tail). They comprise ~1–2% of total pancreatic mass but receive ~15% of pancreatic blood flow.

Cell Type% of IsletHormonePrimary Function
α (Alpha) cells~15–20%Glucagon↑ Blood glucose (glycogenolysis, gluconeogenesis)
β (Beta) cells~65–80%Insulin + Amylin (IAPP)↓ Blood glucose; amylin slows gastric emptying, suppresses glucagon
δ (Delta) cells~3–10%SomatostatinParacrine inhibitor of insulin & glucagon release
PP (F) cells~3–5%Pancreatic Polypeptide↓ Pancreatic exocrine secretion, ↓ gastric motility
ε (Epsilon) cells<1%GhrelinStimulates appetite/growth hormone release
High-Yield: β-cells are the most abundant cell type. They secrete both insulin AND amylin (islet amyloid polypeptide/IAPP). Amylin co-secretion is clinically important — pramlintide is a synthetic amylin analog used in DM management. In T2DM, amylin overproduction → misfolding → amyloid plaques in the pancreas.
LO 2 Compare and contrast the stimulants and inhibitors of insulin release

⚡ Stimulants & Inhibitors of Insulin Secretion

Insulin release from β-cells is tightly regulated by nutrient, hormonal, and neural signals. The primary trigger is elevated blood glucose, which enters β-cells via GLUT2 (insulin-independent transporter).

✅ Stimulants of Insulin Release

  • ↑ Blood glucose (primary stimulus)
  • Amino acids (arginine, leucine, alanine)
  • ↑ Fatty acids
  • Incretins: GLP-1, GIP — glucose-dependent insulinotropic effect
  • Glucagon — stimulates insulin release
  • Parasympathetic (vagal) stimulation (ACh)
  • Sulfonylureas (pharmacologic: block KATP channels)
  • CCK (cholecystokinin)

❌ Inhibitors of Insulin Release

  • ↓ Blood glucose (hypoglycemia)
  • Somatostatin (paracrine from δ-cells)
  • Sympathetic stimulation (α₂-adrenergic activation)
  • Epinephrine/norepinephrine
  • Diazoxide (opens KATP channels)
  • Fasting / starvation
  • Leptin
Glucose-Stimulated Insulin Secretion (GSIS) Mechanism: Glucose enters β-cell via GLUT2 → glucose metabolism → ↑ ATP/ADP ratio → closure of KATP channels → membrane depolarization → opening of voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels → Ca²⁺ influx → exocytosis of insulin granules. This is the exact mechanism that sulfonylureas exploit — they directly close KATP channels independent of glucose.
Clinical Pearl: Glucagon stimulates insulin release, but insulin inhibits glucagon release. This reciprocal relationship is disrupted in diabetes — in T2DM, α-cells become resistant to insulin's suppressive effect, leading to inappropriate glucagon secretion and worsened hyperglycemia.
LO 3 Characterize the insulin receptor and identify the intracellular signaling pathways involved with its activation

🧬 The Insulin Receptor & Signaling Cascade

The insulin receptor is a receptor tyrosine kinase (RTK) that exists as a heterotetrameric complex composed of 2 extracellular α subunits and 2 transmembrane β subunits linked by disulfide bonds. It is not a dimerization event — the receptor pre-exists as a tetramer in the membrane.

Slide 7 — Insulin receptor signaling — PI3K & RAS/MAPK pathways
Insulin receptor signaling — PI3K & RAS/MAPK pathways

Activation sequence:

  • Insulin binds the α subunits → removes their inhibitory constraint on the β subunits
  • Conformational change → autophosphorylation of tyrosine residues on the intracellular β subunits
  • Phosphorylation of Insulin Receptor Substrate (IRS) on tyrosine residues
  • Signal transduction diverges into two major pathways:

PI3K Pathway → Metabolic Effects

  • IRS → PI3K (phosphoinositide 3-kinase) activation
  • → PKB/AKT (protein kinase B) phosphorylation
  • AKT phosphorylates serine/threonine residues on target proteins
  • Key outcome: GLUT4 translocation to cell surface in muscle & adipose
  • Also: ↑ glycogenesis, ↑ lipogenesis, ↓ gluconeogenesis

RAS/MAPK Pathway → Mitogenic Effects

  • Also called SHC pathway or ERK1 pathway
  • IRS/SHC → RAS (rat sarcoma) → MAPK cascade
  • Promotes cell growth, differentiation, gene expression
  • Both PI3K and RAS/MAPK contribute to mitogenic actions
High-Yield: The PI3K → PKB/AKT pathway is responsible for GLUT4 translocation and the major metabolic effects of insulin. GLUT4 is the insulin-dependent glucose transporter found in muscle, adipose, heart, and brain. GLUT2 (liver) is insulin-independent.

Abbreviations from Ochs: RAS = rat sarcoma · PI3K = phosphoinositide 3-kinase · MAPK = mitogen-activated protein kinase · ERK = extracellular signal-regulated kinases · SHC = Src homology 2 domain-containing · PKB/AKT = protein kinase B

LO 4 Recognize the endocrine effects of insulin on the liver, muscle, adipose tissue, and brain

🎯 Tissue-Specific Effects of Insulin

Slide 8 — Insulin metabolic effects — glycogenesis, glycolysis, lipogenesis
Insulin metabolic effects — glycogenesis, glycolysis, lipogenesis
Target TissueGlucose Transporter↑ Stimulated by Insulin↓ Inhibited by Insulin
Liver GLUT2 (insulin-independent entry) Glycogenesis, glycolysis, lipogenesis, protein synthesis Gluconeogenesis, glycogenolysis, ketogenesis
Skeletal Muscle GLUT4 (insulin-dependent) Glucose uptake (via GLUT4), glycogenesis, glycolysis, protein synthesis, amino acid uptake Proteolysis
Adipose Tissue GLUT4 (insulin-dependent) Glucose uptake (via GLUT4), lipogenesis, lipoprotein lipase activity Lipolysis (↓ hormone-sensitive lipase)
Brain GLUT1, GLUT3 (insulin-independent) Satiety signaling, neuronal survival, cognition Appetite (central satiety effect)
High-Yield Summary: Insulin is the master anabolic hormone. It promotes storage: ↑ glycogenesis, ↑ lipogenesis, ↑ protein synthesis. It inhibits breakdown: ↓ glycogenolysis, ↓ gluconeogenesis, ↓ lipolysis. The net effect is lowering blood glucose. Key point: glucose enters the liver via GLUT2 independent of insulin, but insulin regulates what happens to that glucose once inside.
LO 5 Identify the role of glucagon-related peptides on pancreatic islet cells, the liver, and the stomach

🔄 Glucagon & Glucagon-Related Peptides

Glucagon (from α-cells)

  • Binds class B GPCR → coupled to Gαs → ↑ adenylate cyclase → ↑ cAMP
  • Liver: ↑ glycogenolysis, ↑ gluconeogenesis → ↑ hepatic glucose output
  • Pancreas: stimulates insulin release from β-cells
  • Heart: ↑ rate & force of contraction (bypasses β-receptors)
  • Stimulated by: ↓ glucose, amino acids (arginine, alanine), CCK, fasting, intense exercise
  • Inhibited by: insulin, ↑ fatty acids, somatostatin

GLP-1 (Glucagon-Like Peptide-1)

  • Incretin hormone from intestinal L-cells
  • Pancreas (β): ↑ insulin secretion (glucose-dependent)
  • Pancreas (α): ↓ glucagon secretion
  • Stomach: slows gastric emptying
  • Brain: ↑ satiety → ↓ food intake
  • Rapidly degraded by DPP-4 (t½ ~2 min)

GIP (Glucose-dependent Insulinotropic Polypeptide)

  • Incretin hormone from intestinal K-cells
  • Pancreas: ↑ insulin secretion (glucose-dependent)
  • Also degraded by DPP-4
  • Combined GIP + GLP-1 = "twincretin" (tirzepatide)
  • GIP receptor expression is under PPARγ control

Amylin (IAPP)

  • Co-secreted with insulin from β-cells
  • Pancreas (α): suppresses glucagon
  • Stomach: slows gastric emptying
  • Brain: ↑ satiety
  • Production ↓ in T1DM
  • Forms amyloid plaques in T2DM (hyperamylinemia)
The Incretin Effect: Oral glucose produces a greater insulin response than IV glucose at the same blood glucose level. This "incretin effect" accounts for ~50–70% of post-meal insulin secretion and is mediated by GLP-1 and GIP. This effect is diminished in T2DM, which is why incretin-based therapies (GLP-1 agonists, DPP-4 inhibitors) are effective.
LO 6 Compare and contrast the mechanisms underlying hyperglycemia in insulin resistance vs. insulin deficiency

⚔️ Insulin Resistance vs. Insulin Deficiency

FeatureInsulin Deficiency (T1DM)Insulin Resistance (T2DM)
Underlying ProblemAutoimmune destruction of β-cells → absolute lack of insulinTarget tissues (liver, muscle, adipose) have ↓ response to insulin despite normal or ↑ levels
Insulin LevelsVery low to absentInitially HIGH (hyperinsulinemia) → eventually ↓ as β-cells fail
Receptor StatusReceptors are normalPost-receptor signaling defects: ↓ IRS phosphorylation, ↓ PI3K/AKT activity, ↓ GLUT4 translocation
Why Hyperglycemia?No insulin → no GLUT4 translocation, no suppression of gluconeogenesis/glycogenolysisTissues resist insulin → glucose can't enter cells effectively, liver continues gluconeogenesis
Glucagon↑ Unopposed (no insulin to suppress it)↑ α-cells become resistant to insulin's suppressive effect
DKA RiskHIGH — absolute insulin deficiency → unrestrained lipolysis → ketogenesisLOW — enough insulin to suppress lipolysis (but not enough to control glucose)
Key Distinction: In T1DM, the problem is no insulin production. In T2DM, the problem starts as tissues not responding to insulin (post-receptor signaling defect), leading to compensatory hyperinsulinemia, which eventually exhausts β-cells leading to relative insulin deficiency.
LO 7 Define insulin deficiency and insulin resistance in relation to the levels and activity of insulin and its receptors

📖 Definitions

Insulin Deficiency

  • Definition: Insufficient insulin production by pancreatic β-cells to meet metabolic demands
  • Insulin levels: Low to absent
  • Receptors: Normal number and function
  • Cause: Autoimmune destruction (T1DM) or β-cell exhaustion (late T2DM)
  • C-peptide: Low/absent (T1DM) — equimolar with insulin, reflects endogenous production

Insulin Resistance

  • Definition: Normal or elevated insulin levels fail to produce an adequate biological response in target tissues
  • Insulin levels: Normal → HIGH (compensatory hyperinsulinemia)
  • Receptors: May be downregulated; primary defect is post-receptor signaling (↓ IRS, ↓ PI3K/AKT, ↓ GLUT4)
  • Cause: Obesity, ↑ visceral fat, ↑ FFAs, chronic inflammation, physical inactivity
  • C-peptide: Normal to HIGH (early T2DM)
LO 8 Identify the time course for the onset and duration for the biological actions of insulin

⏱️ Insulin Time Course (Biological Actions)

Insulin's biological effects occur on different timescales:

Seconds to Minutes

  • Receptor autophosphorylation and signaling cascade activation
  • GLUT4 translocation to cell membrane (muscle, adipose)
  • ↑ Glucose uptake begins

Minutes to Hours

  • ↑ Glycogen synthesis (glycogenesis)
  • ↑ Protein synthesis
  • ↑ Lipogenesis
  • ↓ Gluconeogenesis (hepatic glucose output falls)
  • ↓ Lipolysis

Hours to Days

  • ↑ mRNA transcription for lipogenic enzymes
  • ↑ Cell growth and mitogenic effects (via RAS/MAPK)
  • ↓ Expression of gluconeogenic enzymes (PEPCK, G6Pase)
Slide 6 — Insulin pharmacokinetic profiles by type
Insulin pharmacokinetic profiles by type
LO 9 Describe the physiological consequences of obesity that lead to type 2 diabetes mellitus

🔁 Obesity → T2DM Progression

The progression from obesity to T2DM follows a well-characterized pathophysiological sequence:

Stage 1: Obesity & ↑ Visceral Fat

Excess visceral adiposity → ↑ free fatty acids (FFAs), ↑ pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6), ↓ adiponectin → chronic low-grade inflammation

Stage 2: Insulin Resistance

FFAs and cytokines impair post-receptor insulin signaling (↓ IRS phosphorylation, ↓ PI3K/AKT) → muscle, liver, and adipose tissue become resistant to insulin → ↓ GLUT4 translocation, ↑ hepatic glucose output

Stage 3: Compensatory Hyperinsulinemia

β-cells compensate by secreting more insulin to overcome resistance → blood glucose remains near-normal (pre-diabetes phase) → fasting hyperinsulinemia

Stage 4: β-Cell Dysfunction & Failure

Chronic demand + glucotoxicity + lipotoxicity + amyloid deposition (IAPP) → β-cells can no longer compensate → insulin secretion declines → overt hyperglycemia → T2DM diagnosed

High-Yield: The hallmark progression is: Obesity → Insulin Resistance → Hyperinsulinemia → β-cell exhaustion → Relative insulin deficiency → T2DM. In T2DM, amylin (IAPP) is overproduced alongside insulin → misfolds → forms amyloid plaques that contribute to β-cell death.
LO 10 Describe the pathway by which exercise regulates glucose uptake separate from insulin

🏃 Exercise-Mediated Glucose Uptake (Insulin-Independent)

Exercise activates a parallel, insulin-independent pathway for glucose uptake in skeletal muscle:

  • Muscle contraction → ↑ AMP/ATP ratio → AMPK activation (AMP-activated protein kinase)
  • AMPK directly stimulates GLUT4 translocation to the muscle cell membrane
  • This pathway does NOT require insulin signaling — it is entirely independent of the insulin receptor, IRS, or PI3K/AKT
  • Also: ↑ Ca²⁺ from contraction activates CaMKII, which further promotes GLUT4 translocation
Why This Matters for DM Management:
  • In T1DM: exercise can lower blood glucose even without exogenous insulin (but requires careful dose adjustment to avoid hypoglycemia)
  • In T2DM: exercise bypasses the defective insulin signaling pathway entirely — glucose enters muscle despite insulin resistance
  • Exercise + metformin is synergistic — both activate AMPK
  • This is why regular physical activity is a cornerstone of T2DM management
LO 11 Compare and contrast the clinical presentation of type 1 and type 2 diabetes mellitus

🔎 T1DM vs. T2DM — Clinical Comparison

Slide 3 — Comparison table — T1DM vs T2DM characteristics
Comparison table — T1DM vs T2DM characteristics
FeatureType 1 DMType 2 DM
OnsetAcute, often childhood/adolescenceGradual, typically adults (but ↑ in youth)
Body HabitusOften lean/normal weightOften overweight/obese
PathogenesisAutoimmune β-cell destructionInsulin resistance → β-cell failure
Insulin LevelsAbsent/very lowInitially ↑ (hyperinsulinemia) → eventually ↓
C-peptideLow/undetectableNormal to high (early)
AutoantibodiesPresent (anti-GAD65, anti-IA-2, anti-insulin, anti-ZnT8)Absent
Ketosis/DKACommon — presents with DKAUncommon (HHS more typical)
SymptomsClassic 3 P's: polyuria, polydipsia, polyphagia + weight lossMay be asymptomatic for years; fatigue, blurred vision, slow wound healing
TreatmentInsulin is REQUIREDLifestyle + oral agents ± insulin
Concordance (twins)~50%~90% (stronger genetic component)
Lab Differentiator: C-peptide is the gold standard for distinguishing T1DM from T2DM. C-peptide is cleaved from proinsulin in equimolar amounts with insulin — it reflects endogenous insulin production. Low/absent C-peptide = T1DM. Normal/high C-peptide = T2DM (or early T2DM). Also: autoantibodies (anti-GAD65) confirm autoimmune etiology in T1DM.
LO 12 Recall the criteria for the diagnosis of diabetes mellitus and the laboratory test used to monitor glycemic control

🧪 Diagnostic Criteria for Diabetes Mellitus

Any ONE of the following criteria (confirmed on a repeat test unless unequivocal hyperglycemia with acute crisis):

TestNormalPre-DiabetesDiabetes
Fasting Plasma Glucose (FPG)<100 mg/dL100–125 mg/dL (IFG)≥126 mg/dL
2-hr OGTT<140 mg/dL140–199 mg/dL (IGT)≥200 mg/dL
HbA1c<5.7%5.7–6.4%≥6.5%
Random Plasma Glucose—≥200 mg/dL + classic symptoms
Monitoring Glycemic Control — HbA1c:
  • HbA1c (glycated hemoglobin) reflects average blood glucose over the past 2–3 months
  • Measures the % of hemoglobin that has been non-enzymatically glycosylated
  • Target for most adults with DM: <7% (ADA recommendation)
  • Each 1% ↓ in HbA1c → ~30% ↓ risk of microvascular complications
  • Limitations: falsely low in hemolytic anemia, blood loss, hemoglobinopathies; falsely high in iron deficiency
LO 13 Identify medications that affect glycemic control

💊 Medications That Affect Glycemic Control

Drugs That ↑ Blood Glucose (Hyperglycemic)

  • Corticosteroids (↑ gluconeogenesis, ↑ insulin resistance)
  • Thiazide diuretics (↓ insulin secretion)
  • β-blockers (mask hypoglycemia symptoms, ↓ insulin secretion)
  • Niacin (↑ insulin resistance)
  • Atypical antipsychotics (olanzapine, clozapine — weight gain, insulin resistance)
  • Protease inhibitors (HIV meds)
  • Calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, cyclosporine)
  • Epinephrine (counterregulatory hormone)

Drugs That ↓ Blood Glucose (Hypoglycemic)

  • Insulin (exogenous)
  • Sulfonylureas (insulin secretagogues)
  • Meglitinides (insulin secretagogues)
  • Alcohol (inhibits gluconeogenesis)
  • Quinolones (fluoroquinolones — stimulate insulin release)
  • Pentamidine (β-cell toxicity → initial insulin release → later deficiency)
LO 14 Identify the mechanism of action of insulin and its effects on metabolic processes

🔑 Insulin MOA — Detailed Review

Slide 7 — Insulin receptor signaling cascade — PI3K & RAS/MAPK
Insulin receptor signaling cascade — PI3K & RAS/MAPK

Full Signaling Cascade (from Dr. Ochs):

Insulin binds α subunits → conformational change → autophosphorylation of tyrosine residues on β subunits → phosphorylation of tyrosine residues on IRS (Insulin Receptor Substrate) → signal transduction through PI3K and RAS/MAPK pathways → phosphorylation of serine/threonine residues by kinases (e.g., PKB/AKT) on target proteins → alters synthesis or activity of enzymes in metabolic and mitogenic processes.

Net Metabolic Effects:

  • ↑ Glycogenesis — promotes glycogen storage in liver & muscle
  • ↑ Glycolysis — promotes glucose utilization
  • ↑ Lipogenesis — promotes fatty acid & TAG synthesis
  • ↑ Protein synthesis — anabolic
  • ↑ GLUT4 translocation — PKB/AKT-mediated insertion of GLUT4 into membranes of muscle & adipose
  • ↓ Glycogenolysis — suppresses glycogen breakdown
  • ↓ Gluconeogenesis — suppresses hepatic glucose production
  • ↓ Lipolysis — inhibits hormone-sensitive lipase
  • ↓ Ketogenesis

Degradation: Insulin is degraded in the liver, muscle, and kidney. ~15–20% of endogenous metabolism occurs in the kidney — patients with ESRD may need lower insulin doses. Renal metabolism of exogenous insulin can be up to 65%.

LO 15 Differentiate between types of insulin and their uses for both type 1 and type 2 diabetes mellitus

💉 Insulin Preparations — Classification

All modern insulin is produced via recombinant DNA technology. Most preparations contain 100 units/mL (U-100). Routes: SC injection, continuous SC infusion (pump), IV (DKA), or inhaled.

Slide 16 — Insulin pharmacokinetic profiles — duration of action chart
Insulin pharmacokinetic profiles — duration of action chart
CategoryAgent(s)OnsetPeakDurationKey Features
Rapid-actingLispro (Humalog), Aspart (Novolog/Fiasp), Glulisine (Apidra)<15 min1–2 hr3–5 hrDon't form hexamers → rapid monomer absorption. Give 15 min before or immediately after meal. Risk: early hypoglycemia
Short-actingRegular (Humulin R, Novolin R)30–60 min2–3 hr6–8 hrGive ~30 min before meal. Forms hexamers in zinc. Risk: late hypoglycemia. Only insulin for IV use (DKA)
IntermediateNPH (Humulin N, Novolin N)2–4 hr4–14 hr10–16 hrCrystalline suspension with protamine — tissue enzymes degrade protamine releasing insulin
Long-actingGlargine (Lantus, Basaglar, Toujeo)4–5 hrFlat/peakless~24 hrSoluble in acid, precipitates at body pH → slow dissolution. Once daily. Do NOT mix
Ultra long-actingDegludec (Tresiba)30–90 minFlat42 hrForms multihexameric chains SC → dissolves very slowly. Once daily. Do NOT mix
InhaledAfrezza<15 min—Ultra-rapidDry powder. NOT a substitute for long-acting. Not for DKA.
Slide 14 — Insulin analogue structural modifications → PK changes
Insulin analogue structural modifications → PK changes
Why Analogs Differ — Structural Modifications:
  • Lispro: proline at B28 reversed with lysine at B29 → disrupts hexamer formation
  • Aspart: proline → aspartic acid at B28
  • Glulisine: asparagine→lysine at B3, lysine→glutamic acid at B29
  • Glargine: 2 arginines added to B-chain C-terminal + glycine for asparagine at A21 → precipitates at pH 7.4
  • Degludec: threonine removed at B30, lysine B29 conjugated to hexadecanoic acid → forms multihexameric chains + albumin binding
LO 16 Relate PK/PD parameters and adverse effects of insulins and other antidiabetic drugs

⚠️ Adverse Effects & PK/PD Pearls

Slide 18 — Adverse effects comparison table
Adverse effects comparison table
Slide 37 — Relative efficacy and adverse effects of antidiabetic agents
Relative efficacy and adverse effects of antidiabetic agents
Drug / ClassKey PKMajor Adverse EffectsClinical Pearls
Insulins (all)SC injection; degraded in liver, muscle, kidneyHypoglycemia (#1), weight gain, injection site reactions, lipodystrophyESRD → ↓ dose needed (↑ renal metabolism of exogenous insulin up to 65%)
Metformint½ 1.5–3 hr; not metabolized; excreted unchanged via kidneyGI upset (diarrhea, nausea), lactic acidosis (rare but serious), B12 deficiencyWeight neutral. No hypoglycemia as monotherapy. CI in severe renal impairment (eGFR <30)
SulfonylureasHepatic metabolism; renal/biliary elimination; t½ variesHypoglycemia (dose-independent), weight gainGlyburide has active metabolites (caution in renal impairment). Glipizide is SUR1-specific (pancreatic)
GLP-1 agonistsSC (except oral semaglutide 0.4–1% bioavail); renal elimination for exenatideGI: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea; pancreatitis (rare); thyroid C-cell tumors (rodent data)Weight loss (up to 27 lbs w/ semaglutide). Oral semaglutide: empty stomach, 4 oz water, 30 min before eating
SGLT2 inhibitors>65% bioavail; O-glucuronidation (UGT); feces/urineUTIs, genital mycotic infections, euglycemic DKA, volume depletion, Fournier gangrene (rare)Weight loss. Cardio/renoprotective (HF, CKD). "gliflozin" = gl + flo (urine flows)
TirzepatideSC; t½ ~5 days (weekly dosing)GI (nausea, diarrhea); similar to GLP-1 RAGreatest weight loss of all DM agents (up to 25 lbs). Dual GIP/GLP-1 agonist
PioglitazoneHepatic metabolismWeight gain, edema, fluid retention, ↑ fracture risk, ? bladder cancerNo hypoglycemia. ↑ HDL, ↓ TGs. CI in HF (NYHA III/IV)
DPP-4 inhibitorsVaries; linagliptin is biliary (no renal adjustment)Generally well-tolerated; nasopharyngitis, ↑ risk of HF (saxagliptin)Weight neutral. No hypoglycemia as monotherapy. DO NOT slow gastric emptying
MeglitinidesRapidly absorbed, rapid-acting; hepatic metabolism; t½ 1–1.5 hrHypoglycemia (less than SUs), weight gainTake before each meal, skip if meal skipped
AG InhibitorsPartially absorbed; acarbose → feces; miglitol → renalFlatulence, bloating, diarrhea↓ Postprandial glucose only. Must take with first bite of food
LO 17 Recall the mechanism of action and indications of glucagon

🚨 Glucagon — MOA & Indications

Receptor: Glucagon binds the glucagon receptor, a class B G-protein–coupled receptor (GPCR) coupled to Gαs → ↑ adenylate cyclase → ↑ cAMP

MOA:

  • Activates glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis in the liver
  • ↑ Hepatic glucose production → ↑ blood glucose
  • Stimulates rate & force of cardiac contraction (bypasses β-receptors)

Indications:

  • Severe hypoglycemia — given SC (most common use) or via nasal spray (Baqsimi, approved 2019)
  • β-blocker–induced cardiac depression — glucagon increases cardiac output by bypassing β-receptors
Pearl: Patients with diabetes continue to produce glucagon. In T2DM, the problem is excessive glucagon secretion due to α-cell resistance to insulin's suppressive effect. This is why drugs that ↓ glucagon (GLP-1 agonists, pramlintide, tirzepatide) are therapeutically valuable.
LO 18 Recognize which drugs belong to each class for type 2 diabetes mellitus

📋 T2DM Drug Class Identification — Master Table

Slide 38 — Drug class summary table from Goodman & Gilman
Drug class summary table from Goodman & Gilman
Drug ClassAgents (Generic → Brand)Stem / Memory Aid
GLP-1 Receptor AgonistsLiraglutide (Victoza), Dulaglutide (Trulicity), Semaglutide (Ozempic SC / Rybelsus PO), Exenatide (Byetta), Orforglipron (Foundayo)"-glutide" / "-natide"
Orforglipron: first nonpeptidal oral GLP-1 RA
SGLT2 InhibitorsBexagliflozin (Brenzavvy), Canagliflozin (Invokana), Dapagliflozin (Farxiga), Empagliflozin (Jardiance), Ertugliflozin (Steglatro)"-gliflozin" = gl + flo (make urine flow)
BiguanideMetformin (Glucophage)Only biguanide. 1st-line T2DM agent
Dual Incretin (Twincretin)Tirzepatide (Mounjaro)GIP + GLP-1 agonist. "Twin incretin"
SulfonylureasGlipizide, Glyburide, GlimepirideStart with "gl-" (Sulfonylureas start with S, so they start with "gl")
Thiazolidinedione (TZD)Pioglitazone (Actos)"-glitazone"
DPP-4 InhibitorsSitagliptin (Januvia), Saxagliptin (Onglyza), Linagliptin (Tradjenta), Alogliptin (Nesina)"-gliptin"
MeglitinidesRepaglinide (Prandin), Nateglinide (Starlix)"-glinide"
α-Glucosidase InhibitorsAcarbose (Precose), Miglitol (Glyset)"Acarbose" = a-carb-ose (blocks carb breakdown)
Amylin AnalogPramlintide (Symlin)Add-on to insulin (T1DM or T2DM)
LO 19 Identify the mechanism of action of drugs used to treat type 2 diabetes mellitus

⚙️ Mechanisms of Action — All T2DM Drugs

Slide 22 — GLP-1 RA and incretin pathway
GLP-1 RA and incretin pathway
Slide 27 — Tirzepatide mechanism of action diagram
Tirzepatide mechanism of action diagram
Slide 29 — Sulfonylurea mechanism — K-ATP channel blockade
Sulfonylurea mechanism — K-ATP channel blockade
Slide 31 — DPP-4 inhibitor mechanism
DPP-4 inhibitor mechanism
Drug(s)Mechanism of Action
Liraglutide, Dulaglutide, Semaglutide
GLP-1 Receptor Agonists		Stimulate GLP-1 receptors → ↑ glucose-dependent insulin secretion, ↓ glucagon secretion, slow gastric emptying, ↑ satiety → weight loss
Bexagliflozin, Canagliflozin, Dapagliflozin, Empagliflozin, Ertugliflozin
SGLT2 Inhibitors		Inhibit SGLT2 in the proximal tubule → block renal glucose reabsorption → ↑ urinary glucose excretion → ↓ blood glucose (insulin-independent)
Metformin
Biguanide		Activates AMPK → ↑ fatty acid oxidation, ↑ glucose uptake, ↑ nonoxidative glucose metabolism, ↓ hepatic gluconeogenesis, ↓ lipogenesis. Also inhibits complex I of ETC in liver (↓ ATP). Increases peripheral glucose uptake (mechanism not well understood)
Tirzepatide
Dual Incretin (GIP/GLP-1 RA)		Dual agonist at GIP and GLP-1 receptors → ↑ insulin secretion (glucose-dependent), ↓ glucagon, slow gastric emptying, ↑ satiety. Engineered from human GIP molecule; higher affinity for GIPR than GLP-1R. Biased cAMP signaling on GLP-1R
Glipizide, Glyburide, Glimepiride
Sulfonylureas		Block ATP-sensitive K⁺ channels (SUR1 subunit) on β-cell membrane → membrane depolarization → Ca²⁺ influx → ↑ insulin secretion (glucose-independent). Glipizide is SUR1-specific; glyburide & glimepiride bind SUR2 too
Pioglitazone
TZD		Activates PPARγ (nuclear receptor on fat & vascular cells) → preadipocyte differentiation, ↑ adipose FFA storage (↓ ectopic lipid), ↑ tissue insulin sensitivity (↑ insulin signaling, ↑ GLUT4), ↓ hepatic glucose production
Sitagliptin, Saxagliptin, Linagliptin, Alogliptin
DPP-4 Inhibitors		Inhibit DPP-4 enzyme → ↓ degradation of endogenous GLP-1 & GIP → ↑ intact incretin levels → ↑ insulin, ↓ glucagon (glucose-dependent). Do NOT slow gastric emptying. NO significant effect on satiety
Pramlintide
Amylin Analog		Synthetic amylin (IAPP) analog → suppresses glucagon secretion, slows gastric emptying, ↑ satiety. Add-on to insulin for T1DM or T2DM
Repaglinide, Nateglinide
Meglitinides		Bind same SUR1 site as sulfonylureas (but with lower affinity) → stimulate insulin secretion. Require glucose to work. Rapid onset, short duration. Take before meals
Acarbose, Miglitol
α-Glucosidase Inhibitors		Competitively inhibit brush border enzymes (maltase, isomaltase, sucrase, glucoamylase) → delay breakdown of sucrose & complex carbohydrates → ↓ postprandial glucose spike
LO 20 Recognize the second messengers related to the mechanism of action of diabetes drugs

🔗 Second Messengers & Signaling Pathways by Drug

Drug(s)Receptor / TargetSecond Messenger / Signaling Pathway
InsulinInsulin receptor (RTK — receptor tyrosine kinase)Tyrosine phosphorylation → IRS → PI3K → PKB/AKT (metabolic)
IRS/SHC → RAS/MAPK (mitogenic)
Key output: GLUT4 translocation
Liraglutide, Dulaglutide, Semaglutide
(GLP-1 RAs)		GLP-1 receptor (class B GPCR)Gαs → ↑ adenylate cyclase → ↑ cAMP → PKA activation → ↑ insulin exocytosis from β-cells
Tirzepatide
(GIP/GLP-1 dual agonist)		GIP receptor + GLP-1 receptor (both class B GPCRs)Gαs → ↑ cAMP at both receptors. Biased agonist: greater effect on cAMP pathway vs. β-arrestin at GLP-1R (β-arrestin internalizes receptor)
Glipizide, Glyburide, Glimepiride
(Sulfonylureas)		SUR1 subunit of KATP channelBlock KATP channels → membrane depolarization → voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channel opening → ↑ intracellular Ca²⁺ → insulin granule exocytosis
Repaglinide, Nateglinide
(Meglitinides)		SUR1 (same site as SUs, lower affinity)Same as sulfonylureas: ↑ intracellular Ca²⁺ → insulin exocytosis
MetforminAMPK (AMP-dependent protein kinase); also complex I of ETC↑ AMP/ATP ratio → AMPK activation → downstream metabolic effects. Also inhibits mitochondrial glycerol phosphate dehydrogenase. No classic "second messenger" — kinase cascade
Pioglitazone
(TZD)		PPARγ (nuclear receptor)Ligand-activated nuclear transcription factor → binds DNA response elements → alters gene transcription. No traditional second messenger — direct gene regulation
Sitagliptin, Saxagliptin, Linagliptin, Alogliptin
(DPP-4i)		DPP-4 enzyme (inhibition)Indirectly ↑ cAMP in β-cells by preserving endogenous GLP-1/GIP → those incretins act via Gαs/cAMP pathway on β-cells
Bexagliflozin, Canagliflozin, Dapagliflozin, Empagliflozin, Ertugliflozin
(SGLT2i)		SGLT2 transporter (proximal tubule)No second messenger — direct transporter inhibition. Blocks Na⁺/glucose co-transport → glucose stays in urine
Pramlintide
(Amylin analog)		Amylin receptor (CTR + RAMP complex)Gαs → ↑ cAMP (in area postrema/CNS for satiety); also modulates intracellular Ca²⁺
Acarbose, Miglitol
(AG inhibitors)		α-glucosidase enzymes (brush border)No second messenger — direct competitive enzyme inhibition in the GI lumen
High-Yield Summary: The major second messenger patterns to know:
• cAMP pathway (Gαs-coupled): GLP-1 RAs, tirzepatide, glucagon, pramlintide
• Tyrosine kinase / PI3K-AKT: Insulin
• Intracellular Ca²⁺: Sulfonylureas, meglitinides
• AMPK kinase cascade: Metformin
• Nuclear transcription: Pioglitazone (PPARγ)
• No second messenger (direct inhibition): SGLT2i, AG inhibitors, DPP-4i (enzyme inhibitors)
FYI New Obesity Drugs on the Horizon (2026)

🆕 Pipeline Agents (from Dr. Ochs)

AgentMechanism
Semaglutide/Cagrilintide (CagriSema)GLP-1 RA + amylin agonist
SurvodutideDual GLP-1 + glucagon receptor agonist
RetatrutideTriple agonist: GLP-1 + GIP + glucagon receptors
Maridebart cafraglutide (MariTide)GLP-1 agonist + GIP antagonist (novel approach)
Orforglipron (Foundayo)First nonpeptidal oral GLP-1 RA (bioavail ~20–40%). FDA approved 4/1/2026 for obesity

Ecnoglutide (biased GLP-1 RA, selectively activates cAMP-biased GLP-1 receptors reducing receptor internalization) is in phase 3 trials.
